data.epi_content:
The girl.

Yes. The girl I had been close with since before I could properly hold a sword, the one I thought I would be betrothed to before meeting Vivian, and the one I had treated cruelly as of late.

She was burning.

Whoosh.

Simultaneously, something began to emerge in my mind, as if years of memories were being forcibly stuffed into my head.

Along with them came the knowledge that time would revert when Erica died.

The question she had just asked me, which I couldn't answer due to anxiety, wasn't just nonsense.

Thoughts bloom.

The reason these memories were surfacing in my mind was because Erica was about to die.

Either she couldn't be saved no matter what I did.

Either way, it meant the same thing.

When a much more mature Vivian than now came with knights to execute Evan, or rather, the disgrace of Strelitz, he didn't simply accept the sword and commit suicide.

Like offering numerous sacrifices, he had offered himself as a sacrifice too.

From the perspective of sacrifices and the majority, through evil magic, he had succeeded in sending several important things to his past self.

Even if he couldn't turn back time, he sent methods to return to the past, the black magic he had researched until his death, and his lifelong wish.

It was a miracle.

A normal god would have either punished such a sacrifice or simply granted moderate power and been done with it.

But gods who accept sacrifices don't end things at such a sensible point.

They are curious, always return something in exchange for sacrifices, enjoy the emotions of humans living below, and love their suffering.

Having read ancient texts to understand their characteristics, Evan made a proposal to the gods while offering sacrifices.

Though he wanted to go back in time to save the woman, he would offer the pain of losing his beloved woman before his eyes, contrary to his goal.

And he would come to them again to repeat this painful journey once more.

Until he could save Erica.

Until he gained the power and knowledge to save her.

The evil god gladly accepted the proposal from this human who, though not a worshipper, offered sacrifices and told a willing story.

Those who walk through adversity have always made for fascinating stories.

And the evil god sent something back to the past that was a bit lacking to be called Evan.

Due to a slight error, sadness enveloped his body on the first day, but there was no better volatile substance than human emotion, so a day was enough time to shake off the sadness—fortunate, if one could call it that.

After the first day, watching himself still acting stupidly, something a bit lacking to be called Evan despaired.

As always, he was stupid and a bookworm who knew nothing beyond the words written in books.

He wanted to somehow save Erica by making himself feel sad when seeing her like on the first day, but there was no way.

He could only repeat the despair and grief of not being able to save her until Erica died.

In the end, Erica was dying again by some new method she had somehow found.

And his foolish self, now filled with emotion, wailed and regretted, begging not to see such a sight, but the thought in his mind whispered:

"Look ahead. You must pay the price."

He tells his stupid and cruel self to accept the sin.

At those words, Evan raises his head and looks forward.

Then he sees the girl burning and smiling before his eyes.

How could someone burning to death smile without screaming?

Only then does Evan realize he's holding a piece of Erica's tongue in his right hand.

It was he who had driven the girl to this point.

Even if water is sprinkled or dirt is piled on, the flames show no sign of extinguishing, only burning more fiercely.
data.epi_content2:
I had thought Erica was getting a little better as we lived normally.

Was she not getting better but rather breaking down, or rather, was I breaking her?

The emotions that had been buried at the bottom of his heart for years, hidden while living as a black magician, make him wail.

He confronts the contradiction that though they weren't his own, they were also his.

It's a misfortune and pain that ordinary people wouldn't experience, but he was destined to become a black magician who would plunge countless people into hell.

His future self had decided so. If Erica died, he too would walk a path of suffering.

It's a good story anyway.

After expressing his emotions greatly this once, he will suppress this terrible and miserable feeling again.

Because he must send it to his next self.

"How many more times will we have to see that death?

We're too stupid and powerless to properly turn back time.

It might be a meaningless act."

Says the soul whose face can't be seen due to the deeply wrapped robe, only the uncut beard fluttering below.

The girl's burning arms began to contract.

White foam rises from her mouth, but she still doesn't scream.

The smell of cooking meat begins to fill the space.

The sound of some people vomiting is clearly heard.

But he didn't bother turning his head to see that.

What mattered was Erica, who was burning to death.

He wanted to cover his nose.

The smell of a burning person is beyond unpleasant—it's utterly horrific.

He wanted to cover his ears.

The sound of skin sizzling as it roasts and clothes crackling as they burn seemed to whisper that the flames would never go out.

He wanted to close his eyes and look away.

It was grotesque how she knelt down as if not feeling any pain while burning, patting him as if telling him not to cry.

Evan knelt down, begged, wailed, and prayed desperately that he didn't want to see this terrible sight.

But the thought doesn't allow him.

When he tries to turn his head slightly, the black magician in the robe grabs Evan's head firmly and fixes his gaze.

When he tries to close his eyes, fingers force them open.

Like a curse engraved on the soul.

"Look ahead. This is the condition we set.

We'll repeat this as many times as Erica repeats time.

If Erica just dies, it will all be in vain."

Evan wanted to somehow deny the dying Erica before his eyes.

But his body, feeling the hot heat, refuses.

So he pleaded pathetically, wringing his hands, to the future thought that would be visible to no one but himself.

It was an overwhelming disgust, reproach, and whirlwind of emotions suddenly encountered in the midst of daily life.

"I want to stop looking. I want to close my eyes.

I want to stop crying. I don't want to see Erica smiling.

I want to stop feeling this way. These aren't even my emotions originally, but emotions you've accumulated over years.

It wasn't a contract I made."
data.epi_content3:
"Don't run away.

These emotions are worthless, vile, and useless if we're judging by value. Endure it.

You'll fail this time too, so your next self will feel these exact same emotions."

Evan wanted to deny it, but he simply couldn't.

What else could you call this wailing while denying what you caused, if not cheap?

He could only despair and accept.

"Don't differentiate. It's just the future anyway.

From now on, you'll carry this moment in your mind and repeat it endlessly.

We must repeat this endlessly and someday return completely to the past.

It's just starting a little earlier. You'll eventually become me."

In the end, the girl burned to death.

Powerless, completely burned, she collapsed to the ground.

Feeling detached from reality, he tried to wake Erica by touching her shoulder.

She crumbled instantly, collapsing into powder.

"Remember. Erica died with a hole in her head.

At first, she died from losing too much blood from her neck and arms.

There might be many others, but this time she burned to death. Remember.

And you must pass these memories and emotions intact to your next self."

The thought engraves its words on Evan's soul.

Like cursing the precious time wasted by initially denying and fleeing from Erica's death.

As if telling him to start the journey to save Erica right now.

"Until when...?"

"Until we save Erica. Or until she dies for certain."

It was a cruel story.

But perhaps not as cruel as himself, who had manipulated a powerless girl as he pleased and driven her to death.

He sat in front of the completely burned Erica until people came to clear the body, and the Crown Prince came to help him up and move him away, recalling her smiling as she burned.

Looking back, Vivian was standing there.

With a dazed expression.

Judging by her bluish fingertips and the red blood dripping from them, she had clearly used too many spells despite her depleted magic.

In that state, it wouldn't be strange if she collapsed right away, but Vivian was staring with wide eyes at what was once called Erica.

"Vivian, let's go."

"Evan, Erica is dead."

To that, Evan replied.

"No, she's not."

He didn't bother adding "because I'll save her."

The Crown Prince was nearby, and if he heard, he would probably assign people to watch him.

Vivian starts to retch.

Fortunately, no smell came from it.

The smell of burned meat already filled the center of the school grounds.

He returns to his room.

And like the future—or rather, like when the thought came—he began what needed to be done without dwelling on Erica's death.
